name: CPS and ANF Intermediate Representations
summary: Two functional-language IR forms making control flow explicit at the term level. CPS (Steele 1978, Appel 1992) gives every call an explicit continuation; ANF (Flanagan et al. 1993) names every intermediate result with let-bindings. Kelsey 1995 showed CPS and SSA correspond. GHC Core, OCaml Flambda, Roc, and Koka all use ANF or ANF-like forms.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Functional-language compilers historically chose IRs that make control flow explicit at the term level rather than encoding it in a CFG. **Continuation-Passing Style (CPS)** gives every function call an explicit continuation argument; **A-Normal Form (ANF)** names every intermediate result with let-bindings and requires every function argument to be trivial.

Kelsey (1995) showed CPS and SSA correspond formally — SSA's φ-functions correspond to CPS's continuation parameters, and SSA's basic blocks correspond to CPS's continuation lambdas. Most modern functional compilers use ANF or an ANF-like form rather than full CPS, trading expressive power for syntactic simplicity.

sections.title: Continuation-Passing Style
sections.content:
CPS represents every function call as taking an explicit continuation: instead of returning a value, a function invokes its continuation with the value. Function calls never return; they tail-call into continuations. Steele's Rabbit compiler for Scheme (1978) and Appel's SML/NJ (1992) are the canonical implementations.

CPS makes all control flow explicit at the term level: non-local returns, exceptions, coroutines, async/await, and effect handlers all express naturally as continuation manipulation. The cost is that the IR has many more lambda terms than the source — every operation introduces a continuation parameter. For optimisers, this can be either a feature (uniform structure) or a bug (more nodes to process).

sections.title: A-Normal Form
sections.content:
Flanagan, Sabry, Duba, Felleisen (1993) introduced ANF as a simpler alternative to CPS. In ANF, every intermediate result is named (`let x = e in ...`) and every function argument is *trivial* (a variable or a constant — never a complex expression). ANF captures the same sequencing guarantees as CPS but without explicit continuation arguments.

ANF's appeal: it's more readable than CPS, easier to map back to source for debugging, and most optimisations (β-reduction, dead-let elimination, common subexpression elimination) have natural ANF formulations. Most modern functional compilers use ANF or ANF-like forms — GHC's Core in let-normal form, OCaml's Flambda, Roc, Koka before its CPS-based effect handlers.
